topics.title: Osteoporosis
topics.html:
[!info]
Osteoporosis = Systemic Skeletal Disease with Low Bone Mass & Microarchitectural Deterioration → Increased Fragility Fracture Risk. DEXA T-Score ≤-2.5 = Osteoporosis. FRAX for 10-Year Fracture Probability. Bisphosphonates 1st Line; Denosumab Alternative; Anabolics (Teriparatide/Romosozumab) for Severe.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Diagnose osteoporosis using DEXA T-scores and clinical risk factors
  • [ ] Apply FRAX tool for 10-year major osteoporotic/hip fracture probability
  • [ ] Apply treatment thresholds (NOF, NICE, ESCEO guidelines)
  • [ ] Prescribe first-line (bisphosphonates) and second-line (denosumab, teriparatide, romosozumab) therapies
  • [ ] Manage glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis (GIOP)
  • [ ] Monitor treatment efficacy and duration (drug holidays)

2. Definition & Diagnosis

WHO Criteria (DEXA T-Scores)

CategoryT-Score (SD from Young Adult Mean)
Normal≥-1.0 SD
Osteopenia-1.0 to -2.5 SD
Osteoporosis≤-2.5 SD
Severe/Established≤-2.5 SD + Fragility Fracture

Measurement Sites: Lumbar Spine (L1-L4) + Total Hip / Femoral Neck (Lowest T-Score Used).

2. Fracture Risk Assessment — FRAX® Tool

FRAX® (WHO) — 10-Year Major Osteoporotic Fracture / Hip Fracture Probability

Input VariablesRequired
Age, Sex, Weight, HeightMandatory
Previous FractureYes/No
Parent Hip FractureYes/No
Current SmokingYes/No
GlucocorticoidsCurrent/Past (>5mg Pred >3mo)
Rheumatoid ArthritisYes/No
Secondary OsteoporosisT1DM, Untreated Hypothyroidism, etc.
Alcohol ≥3 units/dayYes/No
BMD (Femoral Neck)Optional (Improves Accuracy)

Treatment Thresholds (UK / NOGG)

ScenarioIntervention Threshold (10-Year Probability)
Major Osteoporotic Fracture≥7.5% (Age 50-59) → ≥15% (Age 70+)
Hip Fracture≥3% (Age 50-59) → ≥4.5% (Age 70+)

2. Clinical Risk Factors for Fracture

CategoryRisk Factors
Non-ModifiableAge >65, Female, Caucasian/Asian, Family History (Parent Hip Fracture), Previous Fragility Fracture, Early Menopause (<45), Low BMI (<19)
ModifiableLow BMI, Smoking, Alcohol >3 Units/Day, Sedentary, Low Calcium/Vit D Intake, Falls Risk
DiseasesRA, T1DM, Hyperthyroidism, Coeliac, IBD, Chronic Liver/Kidney Disease, Anorexia, Premature Menopause, Premature Andropause

3. Secondary Causes of Osteoporosis (Always Exclude)

CategoryConditions
EndocrineHyperthyroidism, Hyperparathyroidism, Cushing's, Hypogonadism (Male/Female), T1DM, Acromegaly
GICoeliac, IBD, Gastrectomy, Bariatric Surgery, Chronic Pancreatitis
HaematologicalMultiple Myeloma, Lymphoma, Leukaemia
RheumatologicalRA, Ankylosing Spondylitis, SLE
MedicationsGlucocorticoids (>5mg Pred >3mo), Aromatase Inhibitors, AEDs (Enzyme Inducers), PPIs (Long-Term), Heparin, Methotrexate, Depo-Provera, GnRH Analogues
OtherImmobilisation, Organ Transplant, HIV, HIV Meds (TDF), Loop Diuretics

3. Diagnosis & Workup

Essential Investigations

TestPurpose
DEXA (Lumbar Spine + Hip)Gold Standard — T-Score Diagnosis
Lateral Spine X-Ray / VFAVertebral Fracture Assessment (VFA) — Silent Fractures
BloodsCa, Phos, ALP, 25-OH Vit D, TSH, TFTs, FBC, U&E, LFT, FSH/LH/Oestradiol (Women), Testosterone (Men), 25-OH Vit D
Fracture RiskFRAX® (With/Without BMD)
Lateral Spine X-RayIf Height Loss >3cm, Kyphosis, Back Pain

4. Treatment — Pharmacological Therapy

Treatment Indications (NOGG / NICE / ESCEO)

ScenarioTreatment Indicated
Osteoporosis (T ≤-2.5)Yes
Osteopenia (T -1 to -2.5) + FRAX >ThresholdYes
Fragility Fracture (Any T-Score)Yes
Glucocorticoid-InducedYes (If ≥5mg Pred >3mo + T ≤-1.5)

First-Line: Oral Bisphosphonates

DrugDoseAdministrationMonitoring
Alendronate70mg Weekly POFirst-Line; Fast 30min, Upright 30min, Water 200mLDEXA 2-3yr; eGFR if <35
Risedronate35mg Weekly / 150mg MonthlyAlternative (Better GI Tolerance)
Ibandronate150mg Monthly PO / 3mg IV q3moGI Intolerance
Zoledronic Acid5mg IV AnnualAlternative if Oral Contraindicated; eGFR ≥35 (Avoid <35)eGFR ≥35

Bisphosphonate Duration & Drug Holiday

DurationAction
3-5 Years (Oral) / 3 Years (IV)Reassess → Drug Holiday if Low Risk (T-Score >-2.5, No Fracture)
High Risk (Prior Fracture / T ≤-3.0 / Steroid)Continue Beyond 5-6 Years
Drug Holiday MonitoringDEXA q1-2yr; Restart if T-Score ≤-2.5 or Fracture

3. Second-Line / Anabolic Therapies

DrugDoseIndicationAdvantage
Denosumab60mg SC q6mo1st-Line if BP Contraindicated/Intolerant; GIOPNo Renal Adjustment; Potent ↓ Fracture
Teriparatide20µg SC Daily (Max 24 Months)Severe Osteoporosis (T ≤-3.5, Prior Fracture, Failed BP)Anabolic (↑ Bone Formation)
Romosozumab210mg SC Monthly ×12moSevere (Very High Fracture Risk)Anabolic + Antiresorptive
Raloxifene60mg ODPostmenopausal Women; Breast Cancer Risk ReductionSERM
HRTOestrogen ± ProgestogenEarly Postmenopause (<10yr)Symptom Relief + Bone

Teriparatide / Romosozumab Key Points

DrugDurationMonitoringKey Points
TeriparatideMax 24 Months (Rat Osteosarcoma Risk)ALP, Ca²⁺, Renal FunctionAnabolic; ↑ Bone Formation
Romosozumab12 Months Only → Follow with AntiresorptiveALP, Ca²⁺, CV RiskAnabolic + Antiresorptive; CVD Risk (MI/Stroke)

4. Glucocorticoid-Induced Osteoporosis (GIOP)

Risk Stratification

RiskCriteriaAction
High Risk≥5mg Pred >3mo + T-Score ≤-1.5 OR Prior Fracture OR Age >70Start Bone Protection Immediately
Moderate≥5mg Pred >3mo + T-Score >-1.5Consider (If High FRAX)
Low<5mg Pred OR <3moLifestyle + Calcium/Vit D

First-Line for GIOP

DrugDose
Oral Bisphosphonate (Alendronate/Risedronate)First-Line
Zoledronic Acid 5mg IV AnnualIf Oral Contraindicated
Denosumab 60mg SC q6moPreferred if High Fracture Risk / eGFR <35
TeriparatidePreferred if Very High Risk / Prior Fracture (Anabolic Superior in GIOP)
Calcium + Vitamin D Mandatory with All Therapies.

4. Monitoring & Follow-Up

ParameterFrequency
DEXA2-3 Years After Initiation; Then Q2-3yr
HeightAnnual (Vertebral Fracture Screen)
BiochemistryAnnual (Ca, Phos, ALP, 25-OH Vit D, Renal)
AdherenceEach Visit

4. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Osteoporosis DefinitionT-Score ≤-2.5 SD (Lumbar Spine / Femoral Neck)
OsteopeniaT-Score -1.0 to -2.5
FRAX ThresholdMajor Osteoporotic Fracture ≥7.5-15% (Age-Dependent); Hip ≥3-4.5%
First-Line RxAlendronate 70mg Weekly (Oral Bisphosphonate)
Zoledronic Acid5mg IV Annual; eGFR ≥35; Alternative if Oral Contraindicated
Denosumab60mg SC q6mo; No Renal Adjustment; No Drug Holiday (Rapid Bone Loss on Stop)
Teriparatide20µg SC Daily ×24mo Max; Anabolic; Severe Osteoporosis (T ≤-3.5, Fracture)
Romosozumab210mg SC Monthly ×12mo; Anabolic + Antiresorptive; Severe/High Risk
GIOP Threshold≥5mg Pred >3mo + T ≤-1.5 → Treat
Bisphosphonate HolidayAfter 3-5yr (Oral) / 3yr (IV) if Low Risk; No Holiday for Denosumab
Denosumab HolidayContraindicated → Rapid Bone Loss; Transition to Bisphosphonate
TeriparatideAnabolic; Max 24mo; Severe (T ≤-3.5, Prior Fracture)
Romosozumab12 Months Only → Follow with Antiresorptive
DEXA Monitoringq2-3yr After Initiation; Height Annually (Vertebral Fracture)

8. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Osteopenia vs OsteoporosisOsteopenia: T -1 to -2.5; Osteoporosis: T ≤-2.5
T-Score vs Z-ScoreT-Score = vs Young Adult Mean; Z-Score = vs Age/Sex Matched (Use Z in <50yr)
Bisphosphonate HolidayOral: 3-5yr → Holiday; IV Zoledronic: 3yr → Holiday; Denosumab = NO HOLIDAY
Denosumab HolidayContraindicated — Rapid Bone Loss → Transition to Bisphosphonate
Teriparatide DurationMax 24 Months (Rat Osteosarcoma Risk); Anabolic
Romosozumab Duration12 Months Only → Follow with Antiresorptive
FRAX Without BMDValid (But Less Accurate); Add BMD for Accuracy
Zoledronic Acid RenaleGFR ≥35; <35 → Avoid/Reduce dose
Teriparatide Max Duration24 Months Lifetime Max (Rat Osteosarcoma Data)
Romosozumab Duration12 Months Only → Follow with Antiresorptive

9. Mind Map

Osteoporosis
Definition
T-Score ≤-2.5 (DEXA)
Fragility Fracture Risk
Risk Factors
Non-Mod: Age, Female, FHx, Low BMI, Early Menopause
Modifiable: Smoking, EtOH, Low Ca/VitD, Falls, Steroid
Secondary Causes: Endocrine, GI, Meds, Rheum, Haem
Diagnosis
DEXA (Spine + Hip) → T-Score
VFA (Vertebral Fracture Assessment)
FRAX (10yr Fracture Risk)
Exclude Secondary Causes
Treatment Threshold
T ≤-2.5 → Treat
T -1 to -2.5 + FRAX >Threshold → Treat
GIOP: ≥5mg Pred >3mo + T ≤-1.5
First-Line
Oral Bisphosphonate (Alendronate 70mg/wk)
Zoledronic Acid 5mg IV Annual (Alt)
Second-Line
Denosumab 60mg SC q6mo (No Renal Adj; No Holiday)
Teriparatide 20µg SC Daily (24mo Max) — Anabolic
Romosozumab 210mg SC Monthly ×12mo — Anabolic+Antiresorptive
GIOP
≥5mg Pred >3mo + T≤-1.5 → Treat
Teriparatide Preferred (Anabolic Superior)
Monitoring
DEXA q2-3yr; Height Annually (VFA)
Drug Holiday: Oral BP 3-5yr → Stop; Denosumab NO Holiday

9. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point

OsteopeniaT-Score -1.0 to -2.5

Denosumab60mg SC q6mo; No Renal Adjustment; No Drug Holiday

Romosozumab210mg SC Monthly ×12mo; Anabolic + Antiresorptive; Severe
GIOP≥5mg Pred >3mo + T ≤-1.5 → Treat; Teriparatide Preferred
Drug HolidayOral BP: 3-5yr → Holiday; IV Zol: 3yr → Holiday; Denosumab = NO HOLIDAY
Teriparatide Max24 Months Max (Rat Osteosarcoma Risk)
Romosozumab Duration12 Months Only → Then Antiresorptive

topics.title: Paget Disease Of Bone
topics.html:
[!info]
Paget Disease of Bone = Focal Disorder of Bone Remodelling with Excessive Osteoclastic Resorption → Disorganised Osteoblastic Formation. Age >55; Male > Female; UK/Northern Europe Highest Prevalence. 3 Phases: Lytic → Mixed → Sclerotic. ALP = Disease Activity Marker. Bisphosphonates 1st Line (Zoledronic Acid).

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Recognise clinical presentation: bone pain, deformity, deafness, complications
  • [ ] Apply radiological and biochemical diagnosis (ALP, X-Ray, Bone Scan)
  • [ ] Stage disease: Lytic → Mixed → Sclerotic (Burnt-Out)
  • [ ] Apply treatment: Zoledronic Acid 1st Line; Indications for treatment
  • [ ] Recognise complications: OA, Fractures, Nerve Compression, High-Output HF, Osteosarcoma (<1%)

2. Epidemiology & Pathophysiology

FeatureDetails
IncidenceDeclining; ~1-2% of >55yr in UK
Age>55 Years (Rare <40)
SexMale > Female (1.5:1)
GeographyUK/Northern Europe Highest; African/Asian Rare
GeneticsSQSTM1 Mutation (~30% Familial); Viral (Paramyxovirus Hypothesis)
PathophysiologyExcessive Osteoclastic Resorption → Compensatory Disorganised Osteoblastic Formation → Woven Bone (Weak, Deformed)

Three Phases of Paget Disease

PhaseHistologyALPClinical
1. Lytic (Osteolytic)Osteoclasts > Osteoblasts → Osteolysis↑↑↑ (Early Rise)Often Asymptomatic; Bone Pain if Active
2. Mixed (Osteolytic-Osteoblastic)Osteoclasts + Osteoblasts Active↑↑ (Peak)Bone Pain, Deformity, Widening
3. Sclerotic (Osteoblastic/Burnt-Out)Osteoblasts > Osteoclasts → Dense Sclerotic Bone↓ (Towards Normal)Deformity, Fractures, Nerve Compression

2. Clinical Presentation

FeatureFrequencyDetails
Asymptomatic~70%Incidental ALP ↑ or X-Ray Finding
Bone Pain30-40%Deep, Aching, Night Pain (Active Lytic Phase)
Deformity15-20%Bowing Tibia/Femur, Skull Enlargement (Frontal Bossing)
Fractures5-10%Insufficiency Fractures (Long Bones, Vertebrae)
Osteoarthritis20-30%Adjacent Joints (Hip, Knee) — Mechanical Stress
Nerve Compression5-10%CN VIII Deafness (Skull Base), Spinal Stenosis, CN VII Palsy
High-Output Heart Failure<1% (Severe Polyostotic)Arteriovenous Shunting in Vascular Pagetic Bone
Osteosarcoma<1%Malignant Transformation (Late Complication)

2. Biochemical Markers

MarkerFindingSignificance
ALP (Total)Markedly ↑ (Often 3-10x ULN)Best Activity Marker; Disproportionate vs Other LFTs
Bone-Specific ALP (BALP)↑↑More Specific for Bone Turnover
CalciumNormalUsually Normal (Unless Immobilisation/Hypercalcaemia)
PhosphateNormalNormal
PTHNormalNormal
25-OH Vit DNormal/LowNormal (Unless Concomitant Deficiency)
Urinary Hydroxyproline / NTX / CTX↑↑Bone Resorption Markers (If Available)
ALP is Disproportionately Elevated vs Other LFTs (AST, ALT, GGT, Bilirubin Normal).

2. Imaging

ModalityFindings

| Plain X-Ray | Lytic: Osteoporosis Circumscripta (Skull), "Flame-Shaped" Lytic Advancing Edge (Long Bones)

Mixed: Coarse Trabeculae, Cortical Thickening, Bone Expansion

Sclerotic: Dense Sclerosis, "Cotton Wool" Appearance (Skull), Bony Expansion |

Bone Scan (Tc-99m MDP)Intense, Increased Uptake (Polyostotic, Intense "Hot Spots"); Best for Extent Assessment
MRISoft Tissue Extension, Spinal Canal Stenosis, Nerve Compression
CTCortical Thickening, Bone Expansion, Spinal Stenosis Detail

2. Diagnosis

Diagnostic Criteria (Clinical + Biochemical + Radiological)

CriterionRequirement
ClinicalBone Pain, Deformity, Fracture, Deafness, Neurological Signs
BiochemicalALP ↑↑ (Disproportionate); Normal Ca²⁺, PO₄, PTH
RadiologicalLytic → Mixed → Sclerotic Changes on X-Ray/Bone Scan
Bone Scan = Best for Extent (Polyostotic vs Monostotic); X-Ray = Best for Phase (Lytic/Mixed/Sclerotic).

3. Indications for Treatment

IndicationDetails
Symptomatic Bone PainDeep, Aching, Night Pain (Active Disease)
Impending FractureImpending Pathological Fracture (Long Bones)
Neurological ComplicationsNerve Compression (CN VIII, Spinal Cord), Cranial Nerve Palsies
High-Output Heart FailureSevere Polyostotic Disease
Pre-OperativeSurgery on Pagetic Bone (Joint Replacement, Fixation)
Serum ALP >2x ULNAsymptomatic but High Turnover (Prevent Complications)

3. Management

Bisphosphonates (1st Line)

AgentDoseRouteDurationKey Points
Zoledronic Acid5mg IV Single Infusion1st Line (Preferred)Single Dose (Long Duration)Most Potent; Monitor Renal Function (eGFR ≥35)
Risedronate30mg OD × 2 MonthsOral Alternative2-Month CourseLess Potent; Daily Upright 30min
Pamidronate60-90mg IV (Infusion)If Zoledronic UnavailableMultiple InfusionsOutdated by Zoledronic

Monitoring Response

ParameterFrequencyTarget
ALPq3-6 MonthsNormalisation (Remission)
Bone Scan1-2 YearsReduced Uptake
SymptomsEach VisitPain Relief, Functional Improvement

Relapse / Retreatment

ScenarioAction
ALP Rise >50% Above NadirRetreat with Zoledronic Acid 5mg IV
Symptomatic RelapseRetreat
Maximum CoursesNot Defined; Repeated Courses Safe

3. Complications & Management

ComplicationManagement
OsteoarthritisJoint Replacement (Hip/Knee Common); Pre-Op Bisphosphonate
Pathological FractureInternal Fixation (IM Nailing); Post-Op Bisphosphonate
Nerve Compression (CN VIII)Hearing Aids (1st Line); Cochlear Implant (Severe); Surgery Rare
Spinal Stenosis / Cord CompressionDecompressive Laminectomy ± Instrumentation; Pre-Op Bisphosphonate
High-Output Heart FailureDiuretics, Afterload Reduction, Bisphosphonate (Reduces Vascular Shunt)
OsteosarcomaMultimodal: Surgery (Amputation/Limb-Salvage) + Chemo (Cisplatin/Doxorubicin)

3. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Paget DiseaseFocal Bone Remodelling Disorder; Age >55, Male>Female, UK High Prevalence
Three PhasesLytic (Osteolytic) → Mixed → Sclerotic (Burnt-Out)
ALPDisproportionately ↑ (Best Marker); Peak in Mixed Phase
Zoledronic Acid5mg IV Single Dose — 1st Line (Most Potent, Long Duration)
Treatment IndicationsPain, Impending Fracture, Nerve Compression, High-Output HF, ALP >2x ULN
Skull FindingsOsteoporosis Circumscripta (Lytic); "Cotton Wool" Sclerosis (Sclerotic)
Long Bones"Flame-Shaped" Lytic Edge (Advancing Osteolytic Front); Coarse Trabeculae (Mixed)
Bone ScanBest for Extent — Intense Uptake, Polyostotic
CN VIII DeafnessCommon (Skull Base Involvement); Hearing Aids / Cochlear Implant
Osteosarcoma<1% Risk; Late Complication; Multimodal (Surgery + Chemo)
High-Output HFSevere Polyostotic → AV Shunting in Pagetic Bone
Zoledronic Acid5mg IV Single Dose — Preferred; Monitor eGFR (≥35)
Risedronate30mg OD × 2 Months (Oral Alternative)
RelapseALP Rise >50% Above Nadir → Retreat
ALP in PagetDisproportionately ↑ vs Other LFTs; Best Activity Marker

8. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Paget vs OsteoporosisPaget = Focal, ↑ ALP, Deformity; Osteoporosis = Generalised, Normal ALP, Fracture Risk
Paget vs OsteomalaciaPaget = Normal Ca/PO₄/PTH, ↑ ALP; Osteomalacia = ↓ PO₄, ↑ ALP, ↑ PTH
Lytic vs Sclerotic PhaseLytic = ↑↑ ALP, Expanding Lytic Edge; Sclerotic = ↓ ALP, Dense Bone
ALP in PagetDisproportionate ↑ vs Other LFTs (AST/ALT/GGT Normal)
Bone Scan vs X-RayBone Scan = Extent (Polyostotic); X-Ray = Phase (Lytic/Mixed/Sclerotic)
Zoledronic vs RisedronateZoledronic = Single IV Dose (Preferred); Risedronate = 30mg OD × 2mo (Oral)
Osteosarcoma Risk<1% (Late); Multimodal (Surgery + Chemo)
CN VIII DeafnessCommon (Skull Base); Hearing Aids / Cochlear Implant; Surgery Rare
High-Output HFSevere Polyostotic → AV Shunting; Treat with Bisphosphonates + HF Rx
Zoledronic Acid RenaleGFR ≥35 Required; Avoid if <35

9. Mind Map

Paget Disease of Bone
Epidemiology
Age >55; Male>Female; UK High Prevalence
SQSTM1 Mutation (30% Familial)
Three Phases
Lytic: Osteoclastic > Osteoblastic → ↑↑ ALP
Mixed: Both Active → Peak ALP
Sclerotic: Osteoblastic > Osteoclastic → ALP ↓
Clinical
Asymptomatic (70%)
Bone Pain (Deep, Night)
Deformity (Bowing, Skull Enlargement)
Fractures, OA, CN VIII Deafness
High-Output HF (<1%)
Osteosarcoma (<1%)
Biochemistry
ALP ↑↑↑ (Disproportionate vs LFTs)
Ca, PO4, PTH Normal
BALP ↑↑
Imaging
X-Ray: Lytic (Flame Edge) → Mixed → Sclerotic (Cotton Wool)
Bone Scan: Intense Uptake (Best for Extent)
Treatment
Zoledronic Acid 5mg IV Single (1st Line)
Risedronate 30mg OD × 2mo (Oral Alt)
Indications: Pain, Fx Risk, Nerve Comp, HF, ALP>2xULN
Monitoring
ALP q3-6mo (Target Normalisation)
Relapse: ALP Rise >50% Nadir → Retreat
Complications
OA, Fractures, CN VIII Deafness
Spinal Stenosis, High-Output HF
Osteosarcoma (<1%)

9. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Paget DiseaseFocal Bone Remodelling; Age >55; Male>Female; UK Highest
Three PhasesLytic → Mixed → Sclerotic; ALP ↑↑↑ in Mixed Phase
ALPDisproportionately ↑ vs Other LFTs; Best Activity Marker
TreatmentZoledronic Acid 5mg IV Single Dose (1st Line)
IndicationsPain, Fracture Risk, Nerve Compression, HF, ALP >2x ULN
Skull X-RayOsteoporosis Circumscripta (Lytic); "Cotton Wool" (Sclerotic)
Long Bones"Flame-Shaped" Lytic Edge; Coarse Trabeculae; Bowing
Bone ScanIntense Uptake; Best for Extent (Polyostotic)
CN VIII DeafnessCommon; Hearing Aids / Cochlear Implant
Osteosarcoma<1%; Late; Surgery + Chemo (Cisplatin/Doxorubicin)
High-Output HFAV Shunting in Pagetic Bone; Bisphosphonate + HF Rx
Zoledronic Acid5mg IV Single Dose; Monitor eGFR (≥35)
RelapseALP >50% Nadir → Retreat with Zoledronic
ALPDisproportionate ↑ vs AST/ALT/GGT/Bili

topics.title: Renal Osteodystrophy
topics.html:
[!info]
CKD-MBD = Systemic Disorder of Mineral & Bone Metabolism due to CKD. Universal in CKD G4-5D. Triad: Abnormal Ca/PO₄/PTH, Bone Turnover Abnormalities, Vascular Calcification. KDIGO 2017 Targets: PTH 2-9x ULN, Ca 2.1-2.5, PO₄ 0.8-1.5, Ca×P <4.5.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Explain the CKD-MBD pathophysiology cascade (Phosphate → FGF23 → Vit D → Ca → PTH)
  • [ ] Apply KDIGO 2017 targets for PTH, Calcium, Phosphate, Ca×P Product
  • [ ] Select and sequence phosphate binders (calcium-based vs non-calcium)
  • [ ] Select vitamin D analogues for CKD G4-5D (Alfacalcidol, Calcitriol, Paricalcitol)
  • [ ] Apply calcimimetic (Cinacalcet) indications and monitoring
  • [ ] Recognise and manage adynamic bone disease and calciphylaxis

2. Pathophysiology — The CKD-MBD Vicious Cycle

CKD (↓ GFR)
    │
    ├─→ **Phosphate Retention** (↓ Filtered Load)
    │       │
    │       ├─→ **FGF23 ↑** (Osteocyte Response) → ↓ 1α-Hydroxylase → **↓ 1,25-(OH)₂D**
    │       │       │
    │       │       └─→ **↓ Intestinal Ca²⁺ Absorption** → **Hypocalcaemia**
    │       │
    │       └─→ **Direct PTH Stimulation** (Phosphate → PTH Gene Expression)
    │
    └─→ **Hypocalcaemia** (↓ Ca²⁺ Absorption + ↓ Vit D) → **PTH ↑↑** (Secondary HPT)

Key Mediators

MediatorChange in CKDEffect
Phosphate↑↑ (Retention)Direct PTH Stim; FGF23 ↑
FGF23↑↑↑ (Early Rise)↓ 1α-Hydroxylase; ↓ 1,25-(OH)₂D
1,25-(OH)₂D (Calcitriol)↓↓↓↓ Intestinal Ca²⁺ Absorption → Hypocalcaemia
Calcium↓↑ PTH Secretion
PTH↑↑↑ (Secondary HPT)Bone Resorption; Phosphate Excretion

2. Stages of CKD-MBD (KDIGO)

CKD StageGFR (mL/min)PhosphateFGF231,25-(OH)₂DPTHBone Turnover
G3a (45-59)MildNormal↑Normal/↓↑ (1-2x ULN)Normal/High
G3b (30-44)Moderate↑↑↑↓↑↑ (2-5x ULN)High
G4 (15-29)Severe↑↑↑↑↑↓↓↑↑↑ (5-10x ULN)High/Adynamic
G5 (<15) / DialysisFailure↑↑↑↑↑↑↑Absent↑↑↑↑ (10-50x ULN)Adynamic/Mixed

2. KDIGO 2017 Targets (CKD G3-5D)

ParameterTarget (CKD 3-5D)Target (Dialysis)
PTH2-9x ULN2-9x ULN
Corrected Calcium2.1-2.5 mmol/L2.1-2.5 mmol/L
Phosphate0.8-1.5 mmol/L (Stage 3-4)1.1-1.5 mmol/L (Dialysis)
Ca × P Product<4.5 mmol²/L²<4.5 mmol²/L²
25-OH Vitamin D≥30 ng/mL (75 nmol/L)≥30 ng/mL
Alkaline PhosphataseMonitor TrendsMonitor Trends
Note: PTH Target is Wide (2-9x ULN) — Avoid Over-Suppression (Adynamic Bone Disease).

2. Management Algorithm (KDIGO 2017) — Stepwise

CKD-MBD Diagnosed (CKD G3-5, Abnormal Ca/Phos/PTH)
         │
         ▼
STEP 1: **PHOSPHATE BINDERS** (With Meals)
         │
         ├── **Calcium-Based** (Carbonate/Acetate) — **1st Line if Ca²⁺ Normal/Low**
         │       Dose: 0.5-1g with Meals; Max 1.5-2g Elemental Ca/day
         │       Watch: Hypercalcaemia, Vascular Calcification
         │
         ├── **Non-Calcium-Based** (Sevelamer, Lanthanum, Sucroferric Oxyhydroxide)
         │       **1st Line if Ca²⁺ High / Vascular Calcification / Adynamic Bone**
         │       Sevelamer 800-2400mg TDS with Meals
         │
         └── **Ferric Citrate** (Newer; Also Treats Anaemia)
                 Dose: 1g TDS with Meals
                 │
         ▼
STEP 2: **VITAMIN D ANALOGUES** (If PTH Still >9x ULN after Phosphate Control)
         │
         ├── **Alfacalcidol (1α-OH D3)** 0.25-1µg/day (or 1µg Alternate Days)
         ├── **Calcitriol (1,25-(OH)₂D)** 0.25-1µg/day (or 1µg Alternate Days)
         ├── **Paricalcitol** (Selective VDR Agonist) 1-2µg 3x/week (Post-HD)
         │       Advantage: Less Hypercalcaemia/Hyperphosphataemia
         │
         ▼
STEP 3: **CALCIMIMETICS** (If PTH Still >9x ULN Despite Above)
         │
         ├── **Cinacalcet** 30-180mg/day (BD) — CaSR Agonist
         │       Mechanism: ↓ PTH Secretion; Effect: ↓ PTH, ↓ Ca, ↓ Phos
         │       Adverse: Hypocalcaemia, Nausea, QTc Prolongation
         │       Advantage: **↓ PTH, ↓ Ca, ↓ Phos** (Unique Triple Effect)
         │
         └── **Etelcalcetide** (IV, Post-HD) 5-15mg 3x/week (HD Only)

2. Phosphate Binder Selection Algorithm

Patient Profile1st Line BinderRationale
Normal Ca²⁺, No Vascular Calcification, eGFR ≥30Calcium Carbonate/AcetateCheap, Ca²⁺ Supplement
Hypercalcaemia / Vascular Calcification / Adynamic BoneNon-Calcium (Sevelamer / Lanthanum / Sucroferric / Ferric Citrate)Avoids Ca²⁺ Load
Dialysis + HyperphosphataemiaSevelamer / Lanthanum / Ferric CitrateFerric Citrate → Also Treats Anaemia
Key Principle: Avoid Calcium-Based Binders if Ca²⁺ High or Vascular Calcification Present.

2. Vitamin D Analogues in CKD

AgentIndicationDoseAdvantage
Alfacalcidol (1α-OH D3)CKD G3-50.25-1µg/dayRapid 1α-Hydroxylation; Reliable
Calcitriol (1,25-(OH)₂D₃)CKD G4-5, Dialysis0.25-1µg/dayActive Form; Short t½
ParicalcitolCKD G4-5D / Dialysis1-2µg 3x/week (Post-HD)Selective VDR → Less Hypercalcaemia/Phosphataemia
DoxercalciferolCKD G4-510-30µg TIW1α-OH D2 Analogue
Key: Nutritional D2/D3 (Cholecalciferol/Ergocalciferol) INSUFFICIENT in CKD G4-5/Dialysis — Requires Renal 1α-Hydroxylation.

2. Cinacalcet (Calcimimetic)

FeatureDetails
MechanismCaSR Agonist → ↓ PTH Secretion
Dose30-180mg/day (BD); Start 30mg BD, Titrate q2-4wk
Effect↓ PTH, ↓ Ca, ↓ Phos (Unique Triple Effect)
Adverse EffectsHypocalcaemia, Nausea, QTc Prolongation
MonitoringCa²⁺ q1-2wk (Start); PTH q1-3mo; QTc ECG
Etelcalcetide (IV, HD Only)5-15mg 3x/week Post-HD; No Oral Absorption Issues

3. CKD-MBD Management Algorithm (KDIGO 2017)

CKD G3-5 / Dialysis + Abnormal Ca/Phos/PTH
         │
         ▼
STEP 1: DIETARY PHOSPHATE RESTRICTION (800-1000 mg/day)
         │
         ▼
STEP 2: PHOSPHATE BINDERS (With Meals)
         │       (See Selection Algorithm Above)
         │
         ▼
STEP 3: VITAMIN D ANALOGUES (If PTH >9x ULN Despite Phosphate Control)
         │       Alfacalcidol / Calcitriol / Paricalcitol
         │
         ▼
STEP 4: CALCIMIMETICS (If PTH >9x ULN Despite Above)
         │       Cinacalcet (PO) / Etelcalcetide (IV HD)
         │
         ▼
STEP 5: PARATHYROIDECTOMY (Refractory SHPT)
         │       Indications: PTH >800-1000 pg/mL; Ca >2.75; Phos >1.8; Calciphylaxis
         │       Procedure: Subtotal (3.5-Gland) + Autotransplant

3. Adynamic Bone Disease — Over-Suppression

FeatureRecognitionManagement
CluePTH <2x ULN (or <100-150 pg/mL) + Low ALP + Low Bone TurnoverStop/Reduce Vitamin D Analogues & Calcimimetics
RiskFracture ↑ (Low Bone Turnover), ↑ Vascular CalcificationPTH Target 2-9x ULN (Not Lower)
DiagnosisBone Biopsy (Gold Standard); Low PTH + Low ALPPTH Target 2-9x ULN (Not Lower)

3. Calciphylaxis (Calcific Uraemic Arteriolopathy)

FeatureManagement
DiagnosisPainful Violaceous Skin Lesions → Necrosis/Ulceration; Biopsy (Medial Calcification)
Risk FactorsCa×P >4.5, Obesity, Diabetes, Warfarin, High PTH, High Calcium/Phosphate
Emergency ManagementUrgent Parathyroidectomy (If Severe SHPT) + Intensive Wound Care
MedicalSodium Thiosulfate 25g IV 3x/week (Post-HD); Cinacalcet (↓ PTH, Ca, Phos); Bisphosphonates (IV Zoledronic)
Wound CareMultidisciplinary (Nephrology, Dermatology, Surgery, Plastics)
Mortality50-80%

ParameterCKD G3-4CKD G5 / Dialysis

Corrected Calcium2.1-2.5 mmol/L (q3-6mo)2.1-2.5 mmol/L (Monthly)
Phosphate0.8-1.5 mmol/L (q3-6mo)1.1-1.5 mmol/L (Monthly)
Ca × P Product<4.5 mmol²/L²<4.5 mmol²/L²
25-OH Vit D≥30 ng/mL (q6-12mo)≥30 ng/mL (q6mo)
Alkaline PhosphataseAnnual (Bone Turnover)q3-6mo

topics.title: Cerebral Salt Wasting
topics.html:
[!info]
Cerebral Salt Wasting (CSW) = Renal Salt Wasting secondary to Intracranial Disease. Hypovolaemic Hyponatraemia with High Urine Sodium. Key Differentiator from SIADH: Volume Status (Hypovolaemic). Management = Saline Resuscitation + Fludrocortisone (Opposite of SIADH).

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Differentiate CSW from SIADH using volume status assessment
  • [ ] Recognise CSW in the context of intracranial disease
  • [ ] Apply saline resuscitation and fludrocortisone therapy
  • [ ] Identify at-risk patient populations (neurosurgical, SAH, TBI)

2. Pathophysiology

MechanismDescription
Primary EventIntracranial Disease (SAH, TBI, Neurosurgery, Tumour, Infection)
Proposed Mechanisms1. Brain Natriuretic Peptide (BNP) Release → ↓ Renal Na⁺ Reabsorption
2. Sympathetic Overactivity → Renal Vasoconstriction → ↓ Renal Perfusion
3. Impaired Renal Sympathetic Regulation → Loss of Sodium Conservation
ResultRenal Salt Wasting → Volume Depletion → 2nd ADH Release → Hyponatraemia

2. Clinical Context & Risk Factors

Risk FactorDetails
Subarachnoid Haemorrhage (SAH)Most Common Setting (Incidence 10-20% Post-SAH)
Traumatic Brain Injury (TBI)Post-Neurosurgery, Severe TBI
NeurosurgeryEspecially Pituitary/Tumour Resection
Intracranial TumoursPituitary, Craniopharyngioma, Metastases
CNS InfectionsMeningitis, Encephalitis, Brain Abscess
Intracerebral HaemorrhageHypertensive, Amyloid Angiopathy

2. Clinical Presentation

FeatureCSWSIADH (For Comparison)
Volume StatusHypovolaemicEuvolaemic
HyponatraemiaYesYes
Urine Sodium>20 mmol/L (Often >100)>20 mmol/L
Urine Osmolality>100 mOsm/kg>100 mOsm/kg
JVPLowNormal
Skin TurgorDryNormal
Postural BPDrop >20 mmHgNormal
Urine OutputHigh (Polyuria)Low/Normal
Serum Uric AcidHighLow/Normal
Underlying DiseaseIntracranial PathologyMalignancy, Drugs, Pulmonary, CNS

3. Diagnostic Criteria

CriterionCSW Requirement
1. Intracranial DiseasePresent (SAH, TBI, Neurosurgery, Tumour)
2. HyponatraemiaSerum Na⁺ <135 mmol/L
3. Volume StatusHypovolaemic (Low JVP, Dry Mucosa, Postural Hypotension)
4. Urine sodium>20 mmol/L (Often >100)
5. Urine Osmolality>100 mOsm/kg (Inappropriately Concentrated)
6. No DiureticsExclude Recent Diuretic Use
All Criteria Must Be Met for Diagnosis.

3. SIADH vs CSW — Side-by-Side Comparison

FeatureSIADHCSW
Volume StatusEuvolaemicHypovolaemic
JVPNormalLow
Skin TurgorNormalDry
Postural BPNormalPostural Drop >20 mmHg
Urine OutputLow/NormalHigh (Polyuria)
Urine Na⁺>20>20 (Often Higher)
Urine Osm>100>100
Serum Uric AcidLow/NormalHigh
BUN/CreatinineNormal/LowElevated (Pre-renal)
PathophysiologyADH Excess → Water RetentionRenal Salt Wasting → Volume Depletion → ADH Release
ManagementFluid RestrictionSaline Resuscitation (+ Fludrocortisone)
Key: Volume Status is the Key Discriminator — SIADH = Euvolaemic; CSW = Hypovolaemic.

3. Management

Acute Resuscitation

StepAction
1. IV 0.9% NaCl1-2L Bolus → Then 150-200 mL/hr (Goal: Euvolaemia)
2. TargetUrine Output >0.5 mL/kg/h; JVP Normal; Postural BP Normal
3. Fludrocortisone0.05-0.2mg OD (Mineralocorticoid Replacement)
4. Salt SupplementationNaCl Tablets 1-2g TDS (If Ongoing Losses)
5. MonitorNa⁺, K⁺, Volume Status, UOP q2-4h

Duration of Therapy

PhaseDuration
Acute Phase1-2 Weeks Post-Insult (Peak Salt Wasting)
WeaningGradual Over 1-2 Weeks as CNS Recovers
DiscontinuationWhen Euvolaemic + Stable Na⁺ + No Ongoing Salt Loss

3. Complications & Monitoring

ComplicationPrevention / Management
Rapid Na⁺ CorrectionMax 8-10 mmol/L/24h (ODS Risk)
Volume OverloadMonitor JVP, CVP, Lungs; Adjust Fluids
HyperkalaemiaFludrocortisone → Monitor K⁺
HypertensionFludrocortisone → Monitor BP
Recurrent HyponatraemiaContinue Fludrocortisone Until CNS Recovery

3. Differential: CSW vs SIADH vs Other

FeatureCSWSIADHHypovolaemic (GI/Renal)Adrenal Insufficiency
VolumeHypovolaemicEuvolaemicHypovolaemicHypovolaemic
Urine Na⁺High (>20)High (>20)Low (<20) if Extra-renalHigh (>20)
Urine Osm>100>100>100 (If Renal)>100
Postural BPDrop >20NormalDrop (If Severe)Drop
Uric AcidHighLow/NormalNormal/HighNormal/High
CortisolNormalNormalNormalLow
ACTHNormalNormalNormalHigh
Key DiscriminatorHypovolaemia + High Urine Na⁺ + CNS DiseaseEuvolaemiaUrine Na⁺ <20 (Extra-renal)Low Cortisol, High ACTH

3. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
CSW DefinitionRenal Salt Wasting from Intracranial Disease → Hypovolaemic Hyponatraemia
Most Common SettingSubarachnoid Haemorrhage (SAH) (10-20% Post-SAH)
CSW vs SIADHVolume Status = Key Discriminator (CSW = Hypovolaemic; SIADH = Euvolaemic)
CSW Key FeaturesHypovolaemia + High Urine Na⁺ + High Urine Osm + CNS Disease
CSW vs SIADHCSW = Hypovolaemic + Polyuria + High Uric Acid; SIADH = Euvolaemic + Low Urine Output
CSW ManagementSaline Resuscitation + Fludrocortisone (Opposite of SIADH)
Fludrocortisone Dose0.05-0.2mg OD (Mineralocorticoid Replacement)
SAH + HyponatraemiaThink CSW (Peak Day 7-10); Differentiate from SIADH
Post-Operative HyponatraemiaCSW Common Post-Neurosurgery; Differentiate from SIADH
Fludrocortisone Dose0.05-0.2mg OD (Monitor BP, K⁺, Volume)
PathophysiologyBNP Release → Renal Salt Wasting; Sympathetic Overactivity
CSW vs SIADHVolume Status = Key (CSW = Hypovolaemic; SIADH = Euvolaemic)
Uric AcidHigh in CSW (vs Low/Normal in SIADH)
Postural DropCSW: >20 mmHg; SIADH = Normal
CSW Uric AcidHigh (vs Low/Normal in SIADH)

8. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
CSW vs SIADHVolume Status is Key: CSW = Hypovolaemic; SIADH = Euvolaemic
Urine Na⁺ in BothBoth Have High Urine Na⁺ (>20); Volume Status Differentiates
CSW ManagementSaline + Fludrocortisone (Not Fluid Restriction!)
CSW vs SIADHCSW = Hypovolaemic + Polyuria; SIADH = Euvolaemic + Low Urine Output
CSW SettingsSAH > TBI > Neurosurgery > Tumours > CNS Infection
FludrocortisoneMineralocorticoid → Salt Retention; Monitor BP, K⁺
BNP in CSW↑ BNP → Natriuresis (vs SIADH where BNP Normal)
CSW vs SIADHVolume Status: CSW = Hypovolaemic; SIADH = Euvolaemic
Uric AcidHigh in CSW; Low in SIADH
Postural BPCSW: Postural Drop >20 mmHg

9. Mind Map

Cerebral Salt Wasting
Pathophysiology

Renal Salt Wasting → Volume Depletion → ADH Release
Hyponatraemia + Hypovolaemia
Settings
SAH (10-20%)
TBI / Neurosurgery
CNS Tumours / Infection
Clinical
Hypovolaemic Hyponatraemia
High Urine Na+ (>20)
High Urine Osm (>100)
Polyuria, Low JVP, Postural Drop
High Uric Acid
Diagnosis
Hypovolaemic + High Urine Na+ + CNS Disease
Differentiate from SIADH (Euvolaemic)
Management
Saline Resuscitation (0.9% NaCl)
Fludrocortisone 0.05-0.2mg OD
Salt Tablets (NaCl 1-2g TDS)
Monitor: Na+, K+, Vol, BP, Uric Acid
CSW vs SIADH
Volume Status: Hypovolaemic vs Euvolaemic
CSW: Saline + Fludrocortisone
SIADH: Fluid Restriction

9. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point

CSW vs SIADHVolume Status = Key (CSW = Hypovolaemic; SIADH = Euvolaemic)
Most Common SettingSAH (10-20%)
CSW Key FeaturesHypovolaemia + High Urine Na⁺/Osm + Polyuria + CNS Disease
CSW ManagementSaline + Fludrocortisone (Opposite of SIADH Fluid Restriction)
Fludrocortisone0.05-0.2mg OD; Monitor BP, K⁺
SAH + HyponatraemiaThink CSW (Peak Day 7-10); Differentiate from SIADH
Uric AcidHigh in CSW (vs Low/Normal in SIADH)
Postural DropCSW: >20 mmHg; SIADH = Normal
CSW Uric AcidHigh (vs Low/Normal in SIADH)
PathophysiologyBNP Release → Natriuresis; Sympathetic Overactivity
